_meta.description: Chain explainers — how each part of the AI hardware supply chain actually works and where the economic value (and the margin) is captured. 'vals' = approximate share of value/profit captured by layer (illustrative, not precise).

explainers.hk: Leading-edge is a winner-take-most game decided by yield, scale and trust.
explainers.ar: Process R&D → fab capex → yield ramp → fabless customers
explainers.bd: A leading-edge fab costs $20-30B and only pays back at high yield and full utilization. TSMC's edge compounds: more customers fund more R&D, which delivers better yield, which wins more customers. As a pure-play foundry it never competes with its customers (unlike Samsung/Intel), so Apple, Nvidia and AMD all trust it with their crown jewels. The result is ~90%+ of <5nm — a near-monopoly defended by capital intensity, yield know-how and customer trust rather than any single patent.

explainers.hk: AI GPU shipments are gated not by the chip but by how it's packaged.
explainers.ar: GPU die + HBM stacks → silicon interposer (CoWoS) → substrate → module
explainers.bd: An AI accelerator is really many chips (GPU + 8 HBM stacks) mounted on a silicon interposer — TSMC's CoWoS. That interposer capacity, not wafer capacity, has been the binding constraint on Nvidia/AMD output since 2023. TSMC has doubled CoWoS repeatedly and outsourced overflow to ASE/Amkor, yet it stays short. The secondary squeezes are ABF substrates (Ibiden/Unimicron; Ajinomoto's film is a hidden near-monopoly) and hybrid-bonding tools (BESI). Panel-level packaging (FOPLP) is the next-gen escape from round-wafer interposer limits.

explainers.hk: Modern AI is bandwidth-bound — feeding the GPU matters more than the GPU.
explainers.ar: DRAM dies → TSV stacking → base die → HBM stack → on-GPU
explainers.bd: Training large models is increasingly limited by memory bandwidth, not raw compute. HBM solves this by stacking DRAM dies vertically with through-silicon-vias right next to the GPU. It's hard to make (yield-sensitive 3D stacking) and concentrated in Korea: SK Hynix (~62%, Nvidia's lead supplier) leads on the MR-MUF process, with Micron and Samsung behind. All three are sold out through 2026. HBM4 (2026) adds a logic base die fabbed at TSMC — raising the bar and pulling the foundry into the memory business.

explainers.hk: One Dutch company makes the machine that makes every advanced chip.
explainers.bd: EUV lithography prints features a few nanometers wide using 13.5nm light generated by vaporizing tin droplets with a laser. ASML is the only company that can build the system, and within it Zeiss (optics) and Cymer (light source) are single-source. A High-NA EUV tool runs ~$380M and ships in dozens of crates. This stack of monopolies-within-a-monopoly is why export controls on ASML are the West's single most powerful lever over China's chip ambitions — and why there is simply no leading-edge AI silicon without the Netherlands.

explainers.hk: Hyperscalers are building their own chips to escape the Nvidia tax.
explainers.ar: Hyperscaler spec → Broadcom/Marvell co-design → TSMC → in-house deployment
explainers.bd: Nvidia's ~80-90% share and CUDA moat command rich margins, so Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Meta and OpenAI are designing their own accelerators — co-developed with Broadcom (TPU, MTIA, OpenAI) and Marvell (Trainium, Maia). Custom ASICs trade flexibility and software ecosystem for lower cost and power on known workloads (especially inference). Custom shipments are projected +44.6% in 2026 (~28% of AI servers). The irony: whether GPU or ASIC, it's still a TSMC wafer with HBM and CoWoS — so the bottleneck owners win either way.

explainers.hk: Moving bits between chips is starting to cost more power than computing them.
explainers.ar: Pluggable transceiver → on-board optics → co-packaged optics (CPO)
explainers.bd: As clusters scale to 100k+ GPUs, the optical links between them consume a growing share of total power. Today's pluggable transceivers (assembled mostly in China; ~63% share) use power-hungry DSPs and lasers. Co-packaged optics (CPO) moves the optics right next to the switch/GPU die to slash power — Nvidia (Quantum-X/Spectrum-X) and Broadcom are shipping it on TSMC's COUPE silicon-photonics packaging in 2026. The deep chokepoint underneath it all is EML lasers (<5 makers), which is why Nvidia spent ~$4B to lock Lumentum + Coherent supply.

explainers.hk: The hard part of a humanoid isn't the AI — it's the gearbox.
explainers.ar: Reducers + screws + motors → actuators → robot → embodied AI
explainers.bd: A humanoid has ~28-40 actuators, and each joint's costliest parts are precision reducers (harmonic/RV), planetary roller screws and torque motors. These are historically Japanese (Harmonic Drive, Nabtesco) and European (GSA) chokepoints made in low volume at high cost. The whole investment debate is whether China (Inovance, Green Harmonic, Sanhua) can localize and cost-down the actuator BOM fast enough to make humanoids economic — Optimus Gen-3 is reportedly ~70% China-sourced. The pure-plays are in the components, not the robot brands.

explainers.hk: Smart glasses are blocked by the display and the lens — not the software.
explainers.bd: To put a bright image on a transparent lens you need (1) a tiny, ultra-bright, high-PPI micro-display and (2) a waveguide that pipes its light into the eye without washing out in daylight. Neither is solved: microLED is brightest but lowest-yield (JBD); OLEDoS is premium (Sony, in Apple Vision Pro); LCoS is cheap-but-dim (Himax, in the Meta Ray-Ban Display). Waveguides (Lumus reflective; Dispelix/DigiLens diffractive) lose most of their light and need exotic high-index glass or SiC. Until these mature, the component layer is more investable than the device brands.
